title: helix-cli: Staging Merge Conflict Resolution (BLD-448)
description: This tour walks through the conflict resolution run for BLD-448 (Library Comments and Iteration). A staging refresh introduced a reported merge conflict in src/tickets/index.ts between the ticket branch (library CLI integration, 4 commits) and the staging branch (concurrent ticket command updates, 1 commit). The investigation confirmed the conflict was auto-resolved cleanly with zero conflict markers remaining anywhere in src/. No source code changes were required. This tour covers the diagnosis, implementation plan, and verification artifacts that confirm the merged state is correct and the TypeScript build passes.

steps.description: The diagnosis stage identified the root cause: concurrent modifications to the ticket command dispatcher. The ticket branch added library CLI integration while the staging branch independently updated the same dispatcher module (src/tickets/index.ts). merge-conflicts.json listed this single file as conflicted. Crucially, diagnosis confirmed the conflict was already pre-resolved — grep for '<<<<<<<', '=======', and '>>>>>>>' returned zero matches across all .ts files in src/. This means no human or agent intervention was needed to fix markers.
steps.title: Overview: What Triggered This Run

steps.description: The evidence block confirms all expected artifacts of the library CLI feature are present: six command files in src/library/ (index.ts, list.ts, show.ts, comments.ts, comments-list.ts, comments-post.ts), the multi-format item resolver at src/lib/resolve-library-item.ts, and the library dispatch case in src/index.ts around line 98-100. This proves the ticket-side changes survived the merge intact alongside staging's ticket command additions.
steps.title: Evidence Summary: What Was Verified in src/

steps.description: The Assumption-Probe-Learning artifact posed three targeted questions: (1) Are there unresolved markers in tickets/index.ts? (2) Are there stray markers anywhere in src/? (3) Does the resolved file preserve both ticket and staging intents? All three answered cleanly. The 16 grep hits for conflict-like strings were exclusively in .helix/ artifact JSON files from previous runs — not in any TypeScript source file. This scoping distinction is critical to avoid false positives.
steps.title: Diagnosis APL: Three Verification Questions

steps.description: The implementation plan codified four guiding principles: no code changes, verify rather than re-implement, minimal touch (only the 1 file in merge-conflicts.json is in scope), and treat the TypeScript build as the correctness gate. The three-step plan — grep verification, npm install, npm run build — is purely verificatory. This plan prevents an agent from inadvertently modifying already-correct code while still producing a traceable audit trail.
steps.title: Implementation Plan: No Code Changes Required

steps.description: Step 3 of the implementation plan establishes 'npm run build' (tsc) as the primary correctness signal. If the merged tickets/index.ts had any import mismatches, missing handlers, or type errors introduced by the merge, tsc --noEmit would surface them with zero tolerance. This approach is efficient: rather than manually auditing 150 lines of switch statement, the type system validates that all 10 subcommand imports resolve and their call signatures are correct.
steps.title: Build Gate: TypeScript Compilation as Correctness Signal

steps.description: At planning time, Q3 (will the TypeScript build pass?) was marked 'expected yes but not yet executed — deferred to implementation step.' This honest uncertainty was resolved in the subsequent implementation and code review phases, where tsc --noEmit exited with code 0. The APL pattern of flagging deferred verifications prevents premature closure and ensures the build gate is actually exercised rather than assumed.
steps.title: Implementation Plan APL: Build Verification Deferred and Then Confirmed

steps.description: The implementation APL confirms the final outcome: merge-conflicts.json listed src/tickets/index.ts, but the file contained no conflict markers upon inspection. Broad grep across all *.ts files in the repository also returned no conflict markers. No code changes were made. The statement records this as a clean auto-resolved merge, providing an immutable audit record that the conflict resolution run touched no source files.
steps.title: Implementation APL: Conflict Resolution Completed With Zero Changes

steps.description: The code review stage independently verified every claim made by the implementation agent: (1) tickets/index.ts is 150 lines with no conflict markers; (2) all 10 subcommand imports are at lines 4-13 and the switch statement handles all 10 cases; (3) resolveTicket is correctly used for ref-based commands; (4) all 6 library feature files are present; (5) src/index.ts has the library dispatch case. The review verdict is that no code changes were needed, confirming the merge integrity end-to-end.
steps.title: Code Review: Independent Verification of All Claims

steps.description: The code review APL provides the final summary statement: the helix-cli merge from staging was auto-resolved cleanly. No conflict markers remain anywhere in src/. All 10 ticket subcommands (list, latest, get, create, update-description, rerun, continue, artifacts, artifact, bundle) are preserved in tickets/index.ts. The library CLI commands introduced by BLD-448 are intact. tsc --noEmit completed with exit code 0. The codebase's major code paths — ticket dispatch, library dispatch, and the main entry point — are all clean and correctly wired.
steps.title: Summary: All Code Paths Clean, Build Passes
